OCR.z.ai Image.z.ai Audio.z.ai
Design a website where you can register with an email and password, which serves as a prediction site for the 2026 World Cup, including all phases up to the final, and allowing you to choose a team or a draw.

The user wants a 2026 World Cup prediction site with:

  1. Registration with email and password
  2. All tournament phases (group stage, round of 16, quarterfinals, semifinals, final, third place)
  3. Ability to choose a team or draw for each match

Let me plan this out:

Tournament Structure for 2026 World Cup:

  • 48 teams, 12 groups of 4
  • Top 2 from each group + 8 best third-placed teams advance to Round of 32
  • Wait, actually the format was adjusted. Let me recall:
    • 48 teams in 12 groups of 4
    • Top 2 from each group (24 teams) + 8 best third-placed teams = 32 teams in knockout
    • Round of 32 → Round of 16 → Quarterfinals → Semifinals → Third place match → Final

Let me design this with:

  • A sleek, sport-themed dark design (not blue/purple - let's go with a deep green/gold theme reminiscent of football pitches)
  • Registration/Login system with localStorage
  • Group stage prediction tables
  • Knockout stage bracket predictions
  • Ability to pick winner or draw for each match

Color Scheme:

  • Dark background with emerald green accents and gold highlights
  • Football-pitch inspired

Phases:

  1. Group Stage (12 groups × 6 matches = 72 matches)
  2. Round of 32 (16 matches)
  3. Round of 16 (8 matches)
  4. Quarterfinals (4 matches)
  5. Semifinals (2 matches)
  6. Third Place Match (1 match)
  7. Final (1 match)

That's a LOT of matches. Let me think about UX - I'll need tabs/sections to navigate between phases.

For the 48 teams, I need to list all qualified/expected teams. Let me use the actual qualified teams as of what we know. Since this is 2026, I'll include the host nations and reasonable qualified teams based on current qualification status.

Actually, since not all teams are qualified yet, let me use the confirmed hosts (USA, Canada, Mexico) and fill in with likely qualified teams based on FIFA rankings and confederation slots.

Confederation slots for 2026:

  • UEFA: 16 slots
  • CAF: 9 slots
  • AFC: 8 slots
  • CONCACAF: 6 slots (including hosts)
  • CONMEBOL: 6 slots
  • OFC: 1 slot
  • Inter-confederation playoffs: 2 slots

Let me list 48 reasonable teams:

UEFA (16): France, Germany, Spain, England, Portugal, Netherlands, Belgium, Italy, Croatia, Switzerland, Austria, Turkey, Denmark, Poland, Serbia, Sweden

CAF (9): Senegal, Morocco, Egypt, Nigeria, Cameroon, Algeria, Tunisia, Ghana, Ivory Coast

AFC (8): Japan, South Korea, Iran, Saudi Arabia, Australia, Iraq, UAE, Qatar

CONCACAF (6): USA, Mexico, Canada, Costa Rica, Panama, Jamaica

CONMEBOL (6): Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, Colombia, Ecuador, Chile

OFC (1): New Zealand

Inter-confederation (2): Peru, Syria (or similar)

Let me assign groups. I'll create 12 groups:

Group A: USA, Brazil, Sweden, New Zealand Group B: Mexico, Germany, Nigeria, Peru Group C: Canada, Spain, Cameroon, Syria Group D: Argentina, France, Egypt, Costa Rica Group E: Japan, England, Senegal, Panama Group F: South Korea, Portugal, Morocco, Jamaica Group G: Iran, Netherlands, Algeria, Chile Group H: Saudi Arabia, Belgium, Tunisia, Australia... wait Australia is AFC

Let me redo this more carefully with actual draw-like distribution:

I'll spread teams from different confederations across groups. Each group has 4 teams from different confederations (except UEFA which has 16 teams so some groups will have 2 UEFA teams).

Let me just create a reasonable distribution:

Group A: USA (CONCACAF), France (UEFA), Senegal (CAF), New Zealand (OFC) Group B: Mexico (CONCACAF), Germany (UEFA), Nigeria (CAF), Peru (Inter) Group C: Canada (CONCACAF), Spain (UEFA), Cameroon (CAF), Syria (Inter) Group D: Argentina (CONMEBOL), Croatia (UEFA), Egypt (CAF), Costa Rica (CONCACAF) - hmm 2 CONCACAF Group E: Brazil (CONMEBOL), England (UEFA), Morocco (CAF), Japan (AFC) Group F: Uruguay (CONMEBOL), Portugal (UEFA), Ghana (CAF), South Korea (AFC) Group G: Colombia (CONMEBOL), Netherlands (UEFA), Algeria (CAF), Iran (AFC) Group H: Ecuador (CONMEBOL), Belgium (UEFA), Tunisia (CAF), Saudi Arabia (AFC) Group I: Chile (CONMEBOL), Italy (UEFA), Ivory Coast (CAF), Australia (AFC) Group J: Qatar (AFC), Switzerland (UEFA), Poland (UEFA), Jamaica (CONCACAF) Group K: UAE (AFC), Denmark (UEFA), Serbia (UEFA), Panama (CONCACAF) Group L: Iraq (AFC), Austria (UEFA), Turkey (UEFA), Croatia (UEFA) - too many UEFA

Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just create a reasonable but not perfectly accurate grouping. The key point is the UX and functionality, not perfect accuracy of which teams are in which group.

Actually, let me simplify. I'll create 12 groups with 4 teams each, spread across confederations reasonably. The user just needs to predict outcomes.

Let me code this up. Given the complexity, I'll focus on:

  1. Clean auth flow
  2. Tabbed navigation for each phase
  3. Group stage with 12 groups showing match predictions
  4. Knockout bracket visualization
  5. Save predictions to localStorage
  6. Summary/score view

Let me think about the UI flow:

  • Login/Register screen
  • Dashboard with phase navigation (sidebar or tabs)
  • Each phase shows the relevant matches
  • Group stage: table format with dropdown selects
  • Knockout: bracket-style or list format with team selectors

For space, I'll show groups in a grid (4 columns × 3 rows or 3 columns × 4 rows), each group showing 6 matches.

For knockout, I'll do a clean list view with dropdowns for each match, showing the bracket progression.

Let me start coding. This will be a substantial single-file app.

For the design:

  • Dark theme with rich green (#0a1a0f type) background
  • Gold accent (#d4a853 or similar)
  • White text
  • Card-based layout
  • Football-themed decorative elements

Let me write the code now.

Actually, given the massive number of matches (72 group + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 104 matches), I need to be smart about the UI. Let me use a compact card layout for groups.

For each group, I'll show a mini table with 6 match rows, each with two team dropdowns... no wait, the teams are fixed. The user just predicts the result (Home win, Draw, Away win) for each match.

So each group card shows:

  • Group name
  • 6 matches, each showing: Team A vs Team B, and a selector for 1/X/2

For knockout stages, the user selects which team advances (or draw option where applicable... actually in knockout stages there are no draws - someone advances. But the user asked "allowing you to choose a team or a draw" - maybe they mean for group stage matches you pick 1/X/2, and for knockout you pick which team advances).

Let me implement it as:

  • Group stage: predict Home Win / Draw / Away Win for each match
  • Knockout: predict which team advances (select from the two teams)

This makes more sense football-wise.

Now let me think about the data structure:

javascript
const teams = [
// grouped by confederation
];

const groups = [
{ name: 'A', teams: ['USA', 'France', 'Senegal', 'New Zealand'], matches: [...] },
// ...
];

For knockout, the teams aren't known until the group stage completes. So the user should first fill in group stage predictions, and then the knockout stage gets populated based on their predictions... or alternatively, let the user freely pick any team for each knockout match.

Actually, for a prediction site, the typical approach is:

  1. Predict all group stage results
  2. Based on those predictions, determine who advances
  3. Then predict knockout matches

But that's complex to implement dynamically. A simpler approach (and what most prediction sites do) is let the user freely choose from all 48 teams in knockout stages, or better yet, compute the bracket based on group predictions.

I think the best UX is:

  1. User predicts group stage results (1/X/2)
  2. System auto-calculates group standings and determines qualifiers
  3. User then predicts knockout stages with the qualified teams pre-populated

But this adds significant complexity. Let me implement a hybrid:

  • Group stage: 1/X/2 predictions
  • Knockout: user picks from dropdown of all teams (with a note that ideally they'd follow their group predictions)

Actually, let me do it properly - auto-calculate group standings and populate knockout brackets. That would be impressive.

Let me think about standings calculation:

  • Win = 3 points, Draw = 1 point, Loss = 0 points
  • Tiebreakers: goal difference, goals scored, head-to-head (simplified: just use GD then GS)

For the group matches, I need to define which teams play which. In a group of 4:

  • Match 1: Team 1 vs Team 2
  • Match 2: Team 3 vs Team 4
  • Match 3: Team 1 vs Team 3
  • Match 4: Team 2 vs Team 4
  • Match 5: Team 1 vs Team 4
  • Match 6: Team 2 vs Team 3

For goal difference, I need actual score predictions, not just 1/X/2. Hmm, but the user said "choose a team or a draw" which implies just picking the outcome.

Let me go with: user picks outcome (Home/Draw/Away), and for tiebreaker purposes, I'll assign default scores:

  • Home win: 1-0
  • Draw: 0-0
  • Away win: 0-1

This is simple and consistent.

Then standings are calculated, and knockout is populated.

For the Round of 32, the format would be:

  • 12 group winners + 12 runners-up + 8 best third-placed = 32 teams
  • The matchups would be: 1st of A vs 3rd of B/C/D, etc.

This is getting very complex. Let me simplify the knockout format. Actually for 2026, the exact bracket pairing rules are complex with third-place teams. Let me simplify:

  • Round of 32: Group winners vs third-placed teams, Runners-up vs other third-placed teams
  • I'll just create a fixed bracket and populate it with qualified teams

Actually, let me take a step back. The user wants a prediction site. Let me focus on making it look great and be functional, even if the knockout qualification logic is simplified.

Simplified approach:

  1. Group stage: 12 groups, predict 1/X/2 for each of 6 matches
  2. Auto-calculate standings with default scores
  3. Top 2 from each group advance to R16 (simplified - skip R32, go straight to R16 with 24 teams... no that doesn't work)

OK let me think about this differently. The actual 2026 format:

  • 12 groups of 4
  • Top 2 advance (24 teams)
  • 8 best third-placed teams advance (32 total)
  • Round of 32 → R16 → QF → SF → 3rd place + Final

Let me implement the full thing but with simplified third-place qualification logic.

Actually, for simplicity and better UX, let me do:

  • Top 2 from each group → 24 teams
  • 8 best 3rd place teams → 32 teams
  • Round of 32 bracket

For the R32 bracket, I'll use a simplified pairing:

  • Match 1: 1st A vs 3rd B/C/D (best available)
  • Match 2: 1st B vs 3rd A/C/D
  • etc.

This is getting really complex for a single file. Let me take a practical approach:

Revised plan:

  1. Show group stage with predictions
  2. Calculate standings
  3. For knockout, pre-define the bracket slots (like "Winner Group A vs 3rd Place Group B/C/D") and populate them
  4. For the 8 best third-place teams, just take the top 8 by points/GD

Let me code this. I'll organize the code with clear sections.

Let me define the 48 teams and 12 groups now:

Group A: USA, France, Senegal, New Zealand Group B: Mexico, Germany, Nigeria, Peru
Group C: Canada, Spain, Cameroon, Syria Group D: Argentina, Croatia, Egypt, Costa Rica Group E: Brazil, England, Morocco, Japan Group F: Uruguay, Portugal, Ghana, South Korea Group G: Colombia, Netherlands, Algeria, Iran Group H: Ecuador, Belgium, Tunisia, Saudi Arabia Group I: Chile, Italy, Ivory Coast, Australia Group J: Qatar, Switzerland, Poland, Jamaica Group K: UAE, Denmark, Serbia, Panama Group L: Iraq, Austria, Turkey, Norway

That gives us:

  • CONCACAF: USA, Mexico, Canada, Costa Rica, Jamaica, Panama (6) ✓
  • CONMEBOL: Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, Colombia, Ecuador, Chile (6) ✓
  • UEFA: France, Germany, Spain, Croatia, England, Portugal, Netherlands, Belgium, Italy, Switzerland, Poland, Denmark, Serbia, Austria, Turkey, Norway (16) ✓
  • CAF: Senegal, Nigeria, Cameroon, Egypt, Morocco, Ghana, Algeria, Tunisia, Ivory Coast (9) ✓
  • AFC: New Zealand... no NZ is OFC. Let me fix.
    • AFC: Japan, South Korea, Iran, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Iraq, Australia (8) ✓
  • OFC: New Zealand (1) ✓
  • Inter: Peru, Syria (2) ✓

Total: 6+6+16+9+8+1+2 = 48 ✓

Good. Now for the bracket, the 2026 format has specific matchups. Let me use a simplified version:

Round of 32 pairings (based on FIFA's proposed format):

  1. 1A vs 2B (wait, that's not right for 48-team format)

Actually the FIFA format for 2026 knockout is:

  • Winners of groups A-F play runners-up of groups C-H and best 3rd place teams
  • It's a complex seeding system

Let me just create a reasonable bracket:

R32:

  1. Winner A vs 3rd B/C/D
  2. Winner B vs 3rd A/C/D
  3. Winner C vs 3rd A/B/D
  4. Winner D vs 3rd A/B/C
  5. Winner E vs 3rd F/G/H
  6. Winner F vs 3rd E/G/H
  7. Winner G vs 3rd E/F/H
  8. Winner H vs 3rd E/F/G
  9. Winner I vs 3rd J/K/L
  10. Winner J vs 3rd I/K/L
  11. Winner K vs 3rd I/J/L
  12. Winner L vs 3rd I/J/K
  13. Runner-up A vs Runner-up D
  14. Runner-up B vs Runner-up C
  15. Runner-up E vs Runner-up H
  16. Runner-up F vs Runner-up G
  17. Runner-up I vs Runner-up L
  18. Runner-up J vs Runner-up K

Wait, that's 18 matches, but R32 should have 16 matches (32 teams). Let me reconsider.

Top 2 from each group = 24 teams 8 best 3rd place = 8 teams Total = 32 teams = 16 matches

So the pairing would be:

  • 12 group winners vs some opponents
  • 12 runners-up... no, that's 24 + 8 = 32

Actually:

  • Some group winners play 3rd place teams
  • Some group winners play runners-up
  • Some runners-up play 3rd place teams

Let me just create a fixed bracket with slot names:

text
R32 Match 1: Winner A vs Best 3rd (B,C,D)
R32 Match 2: Winner B vs Best 3rd (A,C,D)
R32 Match 3: Winner C vs Best 3rd (A,B,D)
R32 Match 4: Winner D vs Best 3rd (A,B,C)
R32 Match 5: Winner E vs Best 3rd (F,G,H)
R32 Match 6: Winner F vs Best 3rd (E,G,H)
R32 Match 7: Winner G vs Best 3rd (E,F,H)
R32 Match 8: Winner H vs Best 3rd (E,F,G)
R32 Match 9: Runner-up A vs Runner-up B
R32 Match 10: Runner-up C vs Runner-up D
R32 Match 11: Runner-up E vs Runner-up F
R32 Match 12: Runner-up G vs Runner-up H
R32 Match 13: Winner I vs Best 3rd (J,K,L)
R32 Match 14: Winner J vs Best 3rd (I,K,L)
R32 Match 15: Winner K vs Best 3rd (I,J,L)
R32 Match 16: Winner L vs Best 3rd (I,J,K)

Hmm wait, that uses 12 winners + 12 best 3rd + 8 runners-up = 32 but I only have 8 best 3rd place teams total, not 12. Let me reconsider.

The 8 best 3rd place teams come from across all 12 groups. So only 8 of the 12 third-placed teams advance.

Let me revise:

  • 12 group winners
  • 12 runners-up
  • 8 best 3rd-placed teams = 32 teams

For the bracket, I need to assign these 32 teams to 16 matches. A simplified approach:

text
R32:
M1: 1A vs 3rd₁ (one of best 3rd place teams)
M2: 1B vs 3rd₂
M3: 1C vs 3rd₃
M4: 1D vs 3rd₄
M5: 1E vs 3rd₅
M6: 1F vs 3rd₆
M7: 1G vs 3rd₇
M8: 1H vs 3rd₈
M9: 2A vs 2B
M10: 2C vs 2D
M11: 2E vs 2F
M12: 2G vs 2H
M13: 1I vs 2I
M14: 1J vs 2J
M15: 1K vs 2K
M16: 1L vs 2L

Wait, but that uses only 8 third-placed teams in M1-M8, and then M9-M12 use runners-up, and M13-M16 use winners vs runners-up from I-L. That's 12 winners + 8 third-place + 12 runners-up = 32. But M13-M16 has 4 winners + 4 runners-up = 8, and M9-M12 has 8 runners-up, total runners-up = 4+8 = 12 ✓. Total third-place = 8 ✓. Total winners = 8+4 = 12 ✓.

Then R16: M17: Winner M1 vs Winner M9 M18: Winner M2 vs Winner M10 M19: Winner M3 vs Winner M11 M20: Winner M4 vs Winner M12 M21: Winner M5 vs Winner M13 M22: Winner M6 vs Winner M14 M23: Winner M7 vs Winner M15 M24: Winner M8 vs Winner M16

QF: M25: Winner M17 vs Winner M18 M26: Winner M19 vs Winner M20 M27: Winner M21 vs Winner M22 M28: Winner M23 vs Winner M24

SF: M29: Winner M25 vs Winner M26 M30: Winner M27 vs Winner M28

3rd Place: Loser M29 vs Loser M30 Final: Winner M29 vs Winner M30

OK this is getting complex but doable. Let me code this up.

Actually, you know what, this is getting incredibly complex for a single HTML file. The group stage alone has 72 matches, plus the knockout calculations. Let me think about what's reasonable.

I think the key challenge is making this manageable in a single file while still being impressive. Let me:

  1. Keep the full group stage (12 groups × 6 matches)
  2. Simplify the knockout to R16 → QF → SF → Final + 3rd place (skip R32, just take top 2 from each group = 24... no that doesn't divide evenly)

OK, alternative: let me keep R32 but make the UI very compact and tab-based.

Or, even simpler approach that many prediction sites use: let the user freely select teams for knockout stages (not auto-calculated). This is actually how many prediction games work - you predict the group stage results, and separately you predict who you think will advance through each knockout round, regardless of your group stage predictions.

This would be much simpler to implement and still fully functional. Let me go with this approach but also show auto-calculated group standings.

Final plan:

  1. Auth: Register/Login with localStorage
  2. Group Stage: 12 groups, each with 6 matches, predict 1/X/2. Auto-calculate standings table.
  3. Round of 32: 16 matches, user picks winner from dropdown of all 48 teams for each slot
  4. Round of 16: 8 matches, user picks winner
  5. Quarterfinals: 4 matches
  6. Semifinals: 2 matches
  7. Final & 3rd Place: 2 matches
  8. Summary: Shows all predictions

For the knockout, I'll label each slot (e.g., "1st A vs 3rd place" etc.) and let the user pick which team they think will be in that position. Actually, even simpler: just show the bracket with slot labels and dropdowns.

Hmm, but having 48 teams in every dropdown is overwhelming. Let me do the auto-calculation approach for the knockout after all, at least partially.

Compromise plan:

  1. Group stage with 1/X/2 predictions → auto-calculate standings
  2. R32: auto-populated from group standings. User picks winner of each match.
  3. R16: auto-populated from R32 winners. User picks winner.
  4. QF: auto-populated from R16 winners.
  5. SF: auto-populated from QF winners.
  6. Final/3rd: auto-populated from SF winners.

This means the user's flow is sequential and makes logical sense. The knockout matches are pre-filled based on the bracket structure, and the user just clicks to select the winner of each match.

This is the best approach. Let me implement this.

For the 3rd place teams in R32, I'll take the 8 best 3rd-placed teams by points, then GD, then GF.

  • Tab-based navigation

Actually wait - I realize that for knockout stages that depend on earlier results, I need to handle the case where the user hasn't made all group stage predictions yet. In that case, the knockout bracket should show placeholder text like "Complete group stage to unlock" or similar.

Also, I need to handle the case where a group has ties in standings. I'll use: Points → Goal Difference → Goals Scored → alphabetical order as tiebreaker.

For default scores based on 1/X/2 prediction:

  • Home win: 2-0 (to make GD more interesting than just 1-0)
  • Draw: 1-1
  • Away win: 0-2

This gives more varied standings.

Let me think about how to make the group stage UI work. With 12 groups × 6 matches, that's 72 predictions. Each match needs 3 options (1/X/2). I'll use small button groups.

A group card layout:

text
┌─────────────────────┐
│ GROUP A │
│ ┌─────────────────┐ │
│ │ Pos Team P W D L GD Pts │
│ │ 1 USA 3 2 1 0 +3 7 │
│ │ 2 FRA 3 1 1 1 +1 4 │
│ │ 3 SEN 3 0 2 1 -1 2 │
│ │ 4 NZL 3 0 0 3 -3 0 │
│ └─────────────────┘ │
│ USA vs FRA [1][X][2] │
│ SEN vs NZL [1][X][2] │
│ USA vs SEN [1][X][2] │
│ FRA vs NZL [1][X][2] │
│ USA vs NZL [1][X][2] │
│ FRA vs SEN [1][X][2] │
└─────────────────────┘

This should be compact enough to fit in a card.

For knockout matches:

text
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Winner A vs 3rd Place │
│ ┌─────────┬─────────┐ │
│ │ USA │ SEN │ │
│ │ [select]│ [select]│ │
│ └─────────┴─────────┘ │
└─────────────────────┘

The user clicks on the team they think will win. The selected team gets highlighted.

javascript

'USA': '🇺🇸', 'France': '🇫🇷', 'Senegal': '🇸🇳', 'New Zealand': '🇳🇿',
'Mexico': '🇲🇽', 'Germany': '🇩🇪', 'Nigeria': '🇳🇬', 'Peru': '🇵🇪',
'Canada': '🇨🇦', 'Spain': '🇪🇸', 'Cameroon': '🇨🇲', 'Syria': '🇸🇾',

'Ecuador': '🇪🇨', 'Belgium': '🇧🇪', 'Tunisia': '🇹🇳', 'Saudi Arabia': '🇸🇦',

javascript

group: {
'A_0': '1', // group A, match 0: home win
'A_1': 'X', // group A, match 1: draw
// ...
},
r32: {
0: 'USA', // R32 match 0: USA wins
// ...
},

qf: { ... },
sf: { ... },
final: 'Brazil',
third: 'Argentina'
}

One more design consideration: I want the auth page to be really striking. Maybe a dark background with animated gradient or subtle particle-like effect, and a centered glassmorphic card.

For the dashboard, I want the sidebar to have a subtle gradient and the content area to have a subtle grid/dot pattern background.

Let me code it:

I realize this will be extremely long. Let me be smart about it and use template literals efficiently, and avoid unnecessary repetition.

For the groups data, I can generate the match schedule programmatically:

javascript
// For a group of 4 teams [t0, t1, t2, t3]:
// Match 0: t0 vs t1
// Match 1: t2 vs t3
// Match 2: t0 vs t2
// Match 3: t1 vs t3
// Match 4: t0 vs t3
// Match 5: t1 vs t2

This is the standard round-robin schedule for 4 teams.

OK, I'm going to write this now. Let me be efficient.

Actually, I want to make sure the knockout bracket works correctly. Let me trace through the logic:

  1. User predicts all 72 group matches
  2. For each group, calculate standings:
    • Points (W=3, D=1, L=0) using default scores (HW=2-0, D=1-1, AW=0-2)
    • Sort by: Points desc, GD desc, GF desc, name asc
  3. Extract: 12 winners (1st), 12 runners-up (2nd), 12 third-placed
  4. From 12 third-placed, pick top 8 by same criteria
  5. Populate R32 matches
  6. User selects winner for each R32 match
  7. Winners advance to R16, user selects winners
  8. Continue through to Final

For step 4, I need to be careful about which 8 third-placed teams qualify. The actual FIFA rule involves comparing against specific groups, but for simplicity I'll just take the best 8 overall.

For the R32 matchup assignment, I need to pair the 8 best 3rd-placed teams with the group winners. The actual FIFA format assigns specific 3rd-place teams to specific matches based on which groups they came from. For simplicity, I'll just assign them in order of ranking:

3rd[0] vs 1A, 3rd[1] vs 1B, 3rd[2] vs 1C, 3rd[3] vs 1D, 3rd[4] vs 1E, 3rd[5] vs 1F, 3rd[6] vs 1G, 3rd[7] vs 1H

And then the remaining 4 group winners (I-L) play runners-up: 1I vs 2E, 1J vs 2F, 1K vs 2G, 1L vs 2H

And the remaining runners-up play each other: 2A vs 2I, 2B vs 2J, 2C vs 2K, 2D vs 2L

Wait, that gives: 12 winners + 8 third-place + 12 runners-up = 32 teams in 16 matches. Let me verify:

  • M1: 1A vs 3rd[0] → 2 teams
  • M2: 1B vs 3rd[1] → 2
  • M3: 1C vs 3rd[2] → 2
  • M4: 1D vs 3rd[3] → 2
  • M5: 1E vs 3rd[4] → 2
  • M6: 1F vs 3rd[5] → 2
  • M7: 1G vs 3rd[6] → 2
  • M8: 1H vs 3rd[7] → 2
  • M9: 1I vs 2A → 2
  • M10: 1J vs 2B → 2
  • M11: 1K vs 2C → 2
  • M12: 1L vs 2D → 2
  • M13: 2E vs 2I → 2
  • M14: 2F vs 2J → 2
  • M15: 2G vs 2K → 2
  • M16: 2H vs 2L → 2

Count: 12 winners + 8 third-place + 12 runners-up = 32 ✓ All 12 winners used: A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L ✓ All 12 runners-up used: A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L ✓ 8 third-place teams used ✓

Then R16:

  • M17: W(M1) vs W(M9) → 1A's bracket vs 1I's bracket
  • M18: W(M2) vs W(M10)
  • M19: W(M3) vs W(M11)
  • M20: W(M4) vs W(M12)
  • M21: W(M5) vs W(M13)
  • M22: W(M6) vs W(M14)
  • M23: W(M7) vs W(M15)
  • M24: W(M8) vs W(M16)

QF:

  • M25: W(M17) vs W(M18)
  • M26: W(M19) vs W(M20)
  • M27: W(M21) vs W(M22)
  • M28: W(M23) vs W(M24)

SF:

  • M29: W(M25) vs W(M26)
  • M30: W(M27) vs W(M28)

3rd Place: L(M29) vs L(M30) Final: W(M29) vs W(M30)

This is clean. Let me code it now.

One issue: for the R32 labels, I should show meaningful descriptions like "Winner Group A vs Best 3rd Place" rather than just "Match 1". Let me create labels for each R32 match.

OK, I'm going to write the code now. No more planning - let's go.

Let me structure the JS code:

javascript
// ============ DATA ============
const GROUPS = [
{ name: 'A', teams: ['USA', 'France', 'Senegal', 'New Zealand'] },
// ... 12 groups
];

const TEAM_FLAGS = { ... };

const GROUP_MATCH_SCHEDULE = [
[0, 1], [2, 3], [0, 2], [1, 3], [0, 3], [1, 2]
];

const R32_MATCHUPS = [
{ label: 'Winner A vs Best 3rd', slot1: { type: 'winner', group: 'A' }, slot2: { type: 'third', index: 0 } },
// ...
];

// ============ AUTH ============
let currentUser = null;

function getUsers() { return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('wc2026_users') || '{}'); }
function saveUsers(users) { localStorage.setItem('wc2026_users', JSON.stringify(users)); }
function register(email, pass) { ... }
function login(email, pass) { ... }
function logout() { ... }

// ============ PREDICTIONS ============
function getPredictions() { ... }
function savePredictions(pred) { ... }

// ============ GROUP STAGE LOGIC ============
function calcGroupStandings(groupIdx, predictions) { ... }
function getQualifiedTeams(allStandings) { ... }

// ============ KNOCKOUT LOGIC ============
function getR32Teams(qualified) { ... }

Compact standings format:

text
1. 🇺🇸 USA 7pts (+3)
2. 🇫🇷 France 4pts (+1)
3. 🇸🇳 Senegal 2pts (-1)
4. 🇳🇿 NZL 0pts (-3)

This is more compact than a full table. Let me use this format.

And for matches below:

text
USA 2-0 France [1] [X] [2]
SEN 1-1 NZL [1] [X] [2]
...

text
🇺🇸 USA vs 🇫🇷 France [1] [X] [2]

And the standings update based on the selected result.

OK, final decision on group card layout:

text
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│ GROUP A │
│ 1. 🇺🇸 USA 3 2 0 1 +3 6 │
│ 2. 🇫🇷 FRA 3 1 1 1 +1 4 │
│ 3. 🇸🇳 SEN 3 0 2 1 -1 2 │
│ 4. 🇳🇿 NZL 3 0 0 3 -3 0 │
│ ──────────────────────────── │
│ 🇺🇸 USA vs 🇫🇷 FRA [1][X][2]│
│ 🇸🇳 SEN vs 🇳🇿 NZL [1][X][2]│
│ 🇺🇸 USA vs 🇸🇳 SEN [1][X][2]│
│ 🇫🇷 FRA vs 🇳🇿 NZL [1][X][2]│
│ 🇺🇸 USA vs 🇳🇿 NZL [1][X][2]│
│ 🇫🇷 FRA vs 🇸🇳 SEN [1][X][2]│
└──────────────────────────────┘

This is clean and informative. Let me code it.

html

</nav>

</aside>
<!-- Main -->

<div class="progress-bar">...</div>
<div class="user-badge">...</div>
</header>
<div id="content">
<!-- Dynamic content -->
</div>
</main>
</div>

<!-- Toast container -->

OK, let me just write the code now. I've been planning too long.

Let me think about one more thing: the "choose a team or a draw" requirement. In the group stage, I have 1/X/2 buttons. In the knockout, there's no draw - you pick a winner. But the user mentioned "draw" as an option. Maybe for the final they want a draw option? In real football, the final can go to penalties, so technically it's not a draw. But for prediction purposes, some sites do allow predicting a draw (meaning the match goes to penalties and you're predicting the score after 90 minutes).

I think for group stage, 1/X/2 is perfect. For knockout, just pick a winner. The user's requirement is satisfied by the group stage having draw options.

OK, writing the code now. Final time.

Let me also think about password handling. I'll use a simple hash (not secure, but this is localStorage only - no real security needed). Actually, for a demo, I'll just store passwords as-is since it's all client-side. I'll add a note that this is for demo purposes only.

Let me also add password confirmation for registration and basic validation (email format, password length).

Here's my final code structure plan:

text
HTML:
- head (meta, tailwind, fonts, fontawesome, tailwind config, custom styles)
- body
- #auth-screen (login/register form)
- #dashboard (sidebar + main)
- #toast-container
- script (all JS)

CSS:
- Root variables
- Custom scrollbar
- Auth background animation
- Card styles
- Button styles (1/X/2)
- Knockout team selector
- Toast styles
- Mobile responsive overrides
- Transitions

JS:
- Data constants (groups, teams, flags, abbreviations)
- State variables
- Auth functions
- Prediction storage functions
- Group stage calculation
- Knockout team resolution
- Rendering functions (auth, sidebar, each phase, summary)
- Event handlers
- Toast function
- Init

Let me write it all now.

For the background of the auth page, I'll create animated gradient orbs using CSS:

css
.auth-orb {
position: absolute;
border-radius: 50%;
filter: blur(80px);
opacity: 0.3;
animation: float 20s ease-in-out infinite;
}

With 2-3 orbs in green/gold tones.

For the dashboard background, I'll use a subtle dot pattern:

css
.dashboard-bg {
background-image: radial-gradient(circle, rgba(34, 197, 94, 0.05) 1px, transparent 1px);
background-size: 30px 30px;
}

OK here's the full code. Let me write it out completely now. I'll be efficient but thorough.

One thing I want to make sure: the code actually works. Let me trace through the logic mentally:

  1. User opens page → no session → shows auth screen
  2. User registers → stores user in localStorage, creates session, shows dashboard
  3. Dashboard shows group stage by default
  4. User clicks 1/X/2 buttons → predictions saved, standings recalculated and re-rendered
  5. User navigates to R32 → if all group predictions made, shows R32 matches with team names; otherwise shows message
  6. User clicks on a team in R32 match → prediction saved, that team highlighted
  7. User navigates to R16 → if all R32 predictions made, shows R16 with winners from R32
  8. Continue through to Final
  9. Summary shows all predictions

Edge cases:

  • User hasn't completed all group predictions → knockout phases show "Complete previous phase"
  • User changes group prediction → knockout predictions that depend on changed teams should be... tricky. I'll invalidate downstream predictions when group predictions change. Or I'll just let them be stale and show a warning. Actually, the simplest approach: when group predictions change, clear all knockout predictions that reference the changed teams. But that's complex.

Simplest approach: when user changes a group prediction, show a warning that knockout predictions may be outdated. Don't auto-clear them. The user can manually update.

Actually, even simpler: since knockout predictions store team names, if the teams change due to group prediction changes, the stored team name might no longer be in the match. I'll handle this by checking if the stored prediction is valid for the current match teams, and if not, clear it.

OK let me implement this check when rendering knockout matches.

Let me now write the code. I'll aim for roughly 800-1200 lines of clean, well-organized code.

Actually, I want to make sure I handle the "best 3rd place" logic correctly. When calculating which 8 third-placed teams advance:

  1. Get all 12 third-placed teams with their stats
  2. Sort by: Points desc, GD desc, GF desc
  3. Take top 8

This is straightforward.

For the R32 match labels, I'll show descriptive text:

  • "Winner Group A vs Best 3rd Place (1)"
  • "Winner Group B vs Best 3rd Place (2)"
  • etc.
  • "Winner Group I vs Runner-up Group A"
  • etc.

Let me define the R32 matchups more carefully with their labels:

javascript
const R32_MATCHUPS = [
{ label: 'Winner A vs Best 3rd #1', home: { type: 'winner', group: 0 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 0 } },
{ label: 'Winner B vs Best 3rd #2', home: { type: 'winner', group: 1 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 1 } },
{ label: 'Winner C vs Best 3rd #3', home: { type: 'winner', group: 2 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 2 } },
{ label: 'Winner D vs Best 3rd #4', home: { type: 'winner', group: 3 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 3 } },
{ label: 'Winner E vs Best 3rd #5', home: { type: 'winner', group: 4 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 4 } },
{ label: 'Winner F vs Best 3rd #6', home: { type: 'winner', group: 5 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 5 } },
{ label: 'Winner G vs Best 3rd #7', home: { type: 'winner', group: 6 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 6 } },
{ label: 'Winner H vs Best 3rd #8', home: { type: 'winner', group: 7 }, away: { type: 'third', rank: 7 } },
{ label: 'Winner I vs Runner-up A', home: { type: 'winner', group: 8 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 0 } },
{ label: 'Winner J vs Runner-up B', home: { type: 'winner', group: 9 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 1 } },
{ label: 'Winner K vs Runner-up C', home: { type: 'winner', group: 10 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 2 } },
{ label: 'Winner L vs Runner-up D', home: { type: 'winner', group: 11 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 3 } },
{ label: 'Runner-up E vs Runner-up I', home: { type: 'runner', group: 4 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 8 } },
{ label: 'Runner-up F vs Runner-up J', home: { type: 'runner', group: 5 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 9 } },
{ label: 'Runner-up G vs Runner-up K', home: { type: 'runner', group: 6 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 10 } },
{ label: 'Runner-up H vs Runner-up L', home: { type: 'runner', group: 7 }, away: { type: 'runner', group: 11 } },
];

And the bracket connections for subsequent rounds:

javascript
const R16_MATCHUPS = [
{ label: 'R32 Match 1 Winner vs R32 Match 9 Winner', home: { type: 'r32', index: 0 }, away: { type: 'r32', index: 8 } },
{ label: 'R32 Match 2 Winner vs R32 Match 10 Winner', home: { type: 'r32', index: 1 }, away: { type: 'r32', index: 9 } },
// ...
];

OK I think I have enough detail planned. Let me write the actual code now. No more planning.

Let me write it in one go, making sure everything is consistent.

For the Tailwind config, I'll set up custom colors and fonts:

javascript
tailwind.config = {
theme: {
extend: {
fontFamily: {
display: ['Outfit', 'sans-serif'],
body: ['Space Grotesk', 'sans-serif'],
},
}
}
};

And I'll use CSS variables for the color scheme rather than Tailwind custom colors, since I need them in JS-generated HTML too.

Let me write the full file now. I'll be comprehensive.

Actually, I want to reconsider the color scheme one more time. Deep green + gold is nice but might be too "casino-like". Let me go with:

  • Background: very dark charcoal/near-black (#0a0b0d)
  • Cards: dark gray (#14161a)
  • Accent: warm amber/gold (#d4a853)
  • Secondary: muted teal (#2a9d8f) for contrast
  • Text: off-white (#e8e6e1)
  • Muted text: (#6b7280)
  • Border: (#252830)

This is more sophisticated and less "themed". The gold adds warmth and prestige (World Cup trophy association), the teal adds freshness without being the typical blue.

Session management:

  • On register: save user to localStorage, set sessionStorage for current session
  • On login: check localStorage, set sessionStorage
  • On page load: check sessionStorage for current user
  • On logout: clear sessionStorage, show auth screen

Predictions key: wc2026_pred_${email}

OK, writing the complete code now. This is my final attempt - I'll output the full file.

One more consideration: I should make the group stage scrollable/organized well. 12 groups is a lot. On desktop, I'll show a 3×4 or 4×3 grid. On mobile, a single column.

For the grid, I'll use CSS grid:

  • Desktop: grid-cols-3 with xl:grid-cols-4
  • Tablet: grid-cols-2
  • Mobile: grid-cols-1

Each group card will have a max height with internal scroll if needed, but ideally it fits without scrolling.

Let me estimate the height of a group card:

  • Group header: ~40px
  • 4 standings rows: ~28px each = 112px
  • Divider: ~8px
  • 6 match rows: ~36px each = 216px
  • Padding: ~24px top + 16px bottom = 40px
  • Total: ~416px

That's tall but manageable. On a 1080p screen, we can fit 2 rows of cards (832px) with the header.

For desktop with 3 columns × 4 rows, that would require ~1664px of vertical space. That's a lot of scrolling. Maybe I should make the cards more compact.

Let me compress the standings:

  • Use smaller text
  • Combine stats into one line: "USA 3 2 0 1 +3 6"
  • Reduce padding

And for matches:

  • "USA vs FRA [1][X][2]" on one line with smaller buttons

With compression:

  • Group header: 32px
  • 4 rows × 24px = 96px
  • Divider: 4px
  • 6 rows × 32px = 192px
  • Padding: 16px + 12px = 28px
  • Total: ~352px

Still tall. For 4 rows: 1408px + header. That's OK with scrolling.

Alternatively, I could use a tabbed interface within the group stage: show 4 groups at a time with tabs for groups A-D, E-H, I-L. This would reduce the visible content at once.

Let me do this: 3 tabs for group stage: "Groups A-D", "Groups E-H", "Groups I-L". Each tab shows 4 groups in a 2×2 grid on desktop, 1×4 on mobile.

This is much more manageable! Each tab shows 4 groups, each ~350px tall, in a 2×2 grid = ~700px + header. That fits on screen.

Let me implement this with sub-tabs within the group stage phase.

OK, I think I have a solid plan now. Let me write the complete code.

Actually, to simplify even further, let me make the group cards collapsible/expandable. By default, show the standings (collapsed), and click to expand and see the match predictions. This way all 12 groups fit on screen in a grid.

No, that adds complexity. Let me stick with the 3 sub-tabs approach.

Let me finalize and write the code:

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
<title>WC 2026 Predictor</title>
<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Outfit:wght@300;400;500;600;700;800;900&family=Space+Grotesk:wght@300;400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
<script>
tailwind.config = {
theme: {
extend: {
fontFamily: {
display: ['Outfit', 'sans-serif'],
body: ['Space Grotesk', 'sans-serif'],
}
}
}
}
</script>
<style>
:root {
--bg: #090a0c;
--bg-elevated: #12141a;
--bg-card: #181b22;
--bg-card-hover: #1e2129;
--border: #252830;
--border-light: #2f333d;
--accent: #d4a853;
--accent-dim: #a8832e;
--accent-glow: rgba(212, 168, 83, 0.12);
--green: #10b981;
--green-dim: #059669;
--text: #e8e6e1;
--text-sec: #9ca3af;

Here's a complete 2026 World Cup prediction site with full registration, all tournament phases, and team/draw selection:

HTML
Generate index.html
I need it to save the responses by user, allow updating each of the classifieds to the final keys, and allow locking the phase selection after a certain amount of time.

The user wants three key features added to the World Cup 2026 predictor:

  1. Save responses by user - Each user's predictions should be stored separately and persist (already partially done with localStorage keyed by user email, but need to ensure it's robust)

  2. Allow updating classifieds (qualifiers) through to the final - The knockout bracket should be editable even after predictions are made. If a user changes a group stage result, the qualified teams should update, and the knockout predictions that are now invalid should be flagged or cleared, allowing the user to re-pick. The chain of predictions from group → R32 → R16 → QF → SF → Final should be updatable.

  3. Allow locking the phase selection after a certain amount of time - Add a deadline/lock mechanism. After a set time (e.g., before a match or phase starts), the phase should become locked and uneditable. The admin/user can set a deadline per phase.

Let me build this comprehensively. I'll add:

  • A settings/deadline system where each phase has a lock time
  • When a phase is locked, its predictions can't be changed
  • If group stage results change, downstream predictions that reference changed teams should be cleared/highlighted
  • A clear UI for managing deadlines
  • The ability to see which predictions are invalidated when upstream changes happen

Let me think about the lock mechanism:

  • Each phase gets a deadline timestamp
  • By default, I'll set some reasonable defaults (or let the user set them)
  • A "Lock Settings" panel in the sidebar or as a modal
  • When locked, the phase shows as locked and inputs are disabled
  • Visual indicator (lock icon) on locked phases

For the "update classifieds" feature:

  • When group predictions change, recalculate qualifiers
  • Check if knockout predictions still reference valid teams
  • If not, mark them as "invalidated" and allow re-selection
  • The user can go through each knockout phase and update

  • When group stage changes, compute new qualifiers
  • Compare with old qualifiers stored in predictions
  • If different, clear downstream predictions and show warnings
  • Allow the user to re-pick

And "allow locking the phase selection after a certain amount of time" - a timer/deadline per phase.

text
- Auth (register/login) with localStorage

For invalidation: when a group prediction changes, I'll:

  1. Recalculate qualifiers
  2. For each R32 match, check if the predicted winner is still one of the two teams in that match
  3. If not, clear the prediction
  4. Cascade this check through R16 → QF → SF → Final
  5. Show toast about invalidated predictions

I realize this is going to be quite long. Let me make sure I cover everything and the code is clean and working.

Here's my plan for the code structure:

javascript
// Data constants (groups, teams, flags, brackets)
// State (user, phase, groupTab)
// Auth functions (register, login, logout)
// Deadline functions (get/set deadlines, isLocked)
// Prediction functions (get, save, validate, invalidate)
// Group logic (standings, qualifiers)
// Knockout logic (resolve slots, next teams)
// Render functions (auth, groups, knockout, final, summary, settings)
// Event binding
// Init

Let me write it all out now. I'll be very thorough.

One thing I want to handle well: the invalidation cascade. When a user changes a group match result:

  1. Recalculate that group's standings
  2. Recalculate all qualifiers
  3. For R32: check each match's predicted winner - if it's not one of the two teams currently in that match, clear it
  4. For R16: rebuild R16 teams based on (possibly updated) R32 predictions, then check each R16 prediction
  5. Similarly for QF, SF, Final
  6. Save the updated predictions
  7. Re-render
  8. Show toast about how many predictions were invalidated

Actually, for R16 onwards, the teams depend on R32 predictions which may have been cleared. So if R32 match 5 was cleared, then the R16 match that depends on R32 match 5's winner now has an unknown team. In that case, we should also clear the R16 prediction.

Let me implement this carefully.

javascript
function invalidateDownstream(preds) {
const q = getQualified(preds);
const r32Teams = getR32Teams(preds);
// Check R32 predictions
let invalidated = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
const pick = preds.r32[i];
if (pick && pick !== r32Teams[i].home && pick !== r32Teams[i].away) {
delete preds.r32[i];
invalidated++;
}
}
// Check R16 predictions
const r16Teams = getNextTeams(preds.r32, r32Teams, R16D);
for (let i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
const pick = preds.r16[i];
if (pick && pick !== r16Teams[i].home && pick !== r16Teams[i].away) {
delete preds.r16[i];
invalidated++;
}
}
// Check QF predictions
const qfTeams = getNextTeams(preds.r16, r16Teams, QFD);
for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
const pick = preds.qf[i];
if (pick && pick !== qfTeams[i].home && pick !== qfTeams[i].away) {
delete preds.qf[i];
invalidated++;
}
}
// Check SF predictions
const sfTeams = getNextTeams(preds.qf, qfTeams, SFD);

This looks good. Now let me write the complete file.

For the deadline system:

javascript
function getDeadlines() {
return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('wc26_dl') || '{}');
}
function setDeadline(phaseKey, timestamp) {
const dl = getDeadlines();
dl[phaseKey] = timestamp;
localStorage.setItem('wc26_dl', JSON.stringify(dl));
}
function isPhaseLocked(phaseKey) {
const dl = getDeadlines()[phaseKey];
if (!dl) return false;
return Date.now() > dl;
}
function getTimeRemaining(phaseKey) {
const dl = getDeadlines()[phaseKey];
if (!dl) return null;
const diff = dl - Date.now();
if (diff <= 0) return 'Locked';
// Format as days, hours, minutes
const d = Math.floor(diff / 86400000);
const h = Math.floor((diff % 86400000) / 3600000);
const m = Math.floor((diff % 3600000) / 60000);
if (d > 0) return `${d}d ${h}h`;
if (h > 0) return `${h}h ${m}m`;
return `${m}m`;
}

I need to make sure the complete rendering works. Let me trace through a scenario:

  1. User registers/logs in

  5. Makes R32 predictions
  6. Goes to R16 - sees winners from R32
  7. Makes R16 predictions
  8. Continues to Final
  9. Goes to Summary - sees full bracket

Then: 10. Goes back to Group Stage, changes a prediction 11. System detects qualifier change 12. Clears affected downstream predictions 13. Shows toast "3 predictions invalidated" 14. User goes through phases and re-makes predictions

And for deadlines: 15. User goes to Deadlines settings 16. Sets a deadline for Group Stage to tomorrow 17. After tomorrow, Group Stage shows as locked 18. Can't change any group predictions

OK, I'm going to write this now. Final code coming up.

Let me think about the complete HTML structure and make sure I don't miss anything.

For the knockout rendering, I need to handle the case where upstream predictions haven't been made yet. In that case, the team names might be "?" or the actual team from a previous round. I need to make sure the UI handles this gracefully.

Actually, in my current logic, resolveSlot returns '?' if the slot can't be resolved (e.g., if group predictions aren't complete). And getNextTeams uses the predicted winner or falls back to the home team. Let me make sure this is robust.

Wait, there's an issue with getNextTeams. If a user hasn't predicted a match yet, I'm falling back to the home team, which is misleading. Instead, I should show "?" or indicate that the match is pending.

Let me fix getNextTeams:

javascript
function getNextTeams(prevPreds, prevTeams, bracket) {
return bracket.map(([h, a]) => {
const hn = prevPreds[h]; // predicted winner of match h
const an = prevPreds[a]; // predicted winner of match a
const home = hn || null;
const away = an || null;
const lbHome = home || prevTeams[h].home;
const lbAway = away || prevTeams[a].away;
return { home: lbHome, away: lbAway, lb: lbHome + ' vs ' + lbAway, pendingHome: !hn, pendingAway: !an };
});
}

And in the render, if a team is null (pending), show "TBD" and disable the selection.

Actually, this gets complex. Let me simplify: I'll just show the teams as they would be based on current predictions. If a prediction is missing, I'll show the team name with a "TBD" indicator. The user can only select between the two available teams.

Let me think about this differently. For R32:

  • Teams come from group qualifiers
  • If groups aren't complete, some slots show "?"
  • User can only predict matches where both teams are known

For R16:

  • Teams come from R32 predictions
  • If R32 match isn't predicted, the winner is unknown
  • User can only predict R16 matches where both winners are known

This is the cleanest approach. Let me implement it this way.

javascript
function getR32Teams(preds) {
if (!isGroupComplete(preds)) return null; // Can't show R32
const q = getQualified(preds);
return R32D.map(m => ({
home: resolveSlot(m.h, q),
away: resolveSlot(m.a, q),
lb: resolveSlot(m.h, q) + ' vs ' + resolveSlot(m.a, q)
}));
}

function getNextTeams(prevPreds, prevTeams, bracket) {
return bracket.map(([h, a]) => {
const hn = prevPreds[h];
const an = prevPreds[a];
if (!hn || !an) return null; // Can't resolve this match
return {
home: hn,
away: an,
lb: hn + ' vs ' + an
};
});
}

And in the render, if teams is null or contains null entries, show appropriate messages.

Actually, I think the cleaner approach is:

  • For R32: show all 16 matches once groups are complete, with proper team names
  • For R16: show all 8 matches once all R32 are predicted
  • And so on

If not all upstream are complete, show a lock message saying "Predict all R32 matches first" etc.

But what if only SOME R32 matches are predicted? Then only SOME R16 matches can be predicted. Let me handle this case too - show R16 matches where both teams are known, and grey out the others.

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me keep it simpler for the UI:

  • R32: Available when all groups are predicted
  • R16: Available when all R32 matches are predicted
  • QF: Available when all R16 matches are predicted
  • etc.

This is cleaner and easier to understand. I was already doing this with the isPhaseComplete checks.

But wait, the user said "allow updating each of the classifieds to the final keys". This implies they want to be able to change group results and have the knockout update. So when they change a group result, the R32 should show the new teams, and any R32 predictions that are now invalid should be cleared. Then when they go to R16, the teams should reflect the (updated) R32 predictions.

I think my invalidation cascade handles this. Let me just make sure the rendering shows the current state correctly.

OK, I'm going to write the final code now. Let me be thorough but not overthink this.

Let me also think about what "classifieds" means in this context. In Spanish (the user might be Spanish-speaking based on the phrasing), "clasificados" means "qualified teams". So "allow updating each of the classifieds" means: allow changing which teams qualify from groups, and have that propagate through the knockout.

This is exactly what my system does. When group predictions change → qualifiers change → R32 predictions may be invalidated → cascade continues.

One more consideration: the "locking after a certain amount of time" feature. I'll implement it as:

  1. A "Deadlines" settings page accessible from the sidebar
  2. Each phase has a date/time input
  3. A "Lock Now" button per phase (sets deadline to now)

For the "Unlock" feature: if the user manually locked it (deadline set to past), they should be able to undo it as long as they're the one who set it. Actually, to keep it simple: unlocking just removes the deadline. If the deadline hasn't passed yet, you can remove it. If it has passed, you can't.

Actually wait, "Lock Now" sets the deadline to the current timestamp. So immediately after, the deadline HAS passed. That means you can never unlock it. That's too strict.

Let me reconsider: instead of a datetime, use a simpler approach:

  • Each phase has a lock state and an optional deadline
  • "Lock Now" sets locked=true
  • "Unlock" sets locked=false
  • "Set Deadline" uses a datetime picker
  • When deadline passes, locked becomes true and can't be undone

This is more flexible. Let me store it as:

javascript
{ groups: { locked: false, deadline: null }, r32: { locked: false, deadline: 1234567890 }, ... }

OK let me just code this now. I've been planning long enough.

Actually, to simplify even further: just store deadlines as timestamps. If a deadline is set and has passed, the phase is locked. A "Lock Now" button sets the deadline to 1 second ago. A "Remove Deadline" button removes the deadline (only if it hasn't passed yet).

Here goes:

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
<title>WC 2026 Predictor</title>
<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Outfit:wght@300;400;500;600;700;800;900&family=Space+Grotesk:wght@300;400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
<script>
tailwind.config = { theme: { extend: { fontFamily: { display: ['Outfit'], body: ['Space Grotesk'] } } } }
</script>
<style>
/* ... all styles ... */
</style>
</head>
<body>
<div id="app"></div>
<div id="toasts" class="toast-c"></div>
<script>
/* ... all JS ... */
</script>
</body>
</html>

Let me write the complete code now. I'll focus on making it work correctly and look great.

For the summary/bracket view, I'll create a nice visual representation. Let me think about this...

Actually, for a really nice bracket, I could use a CSS grid approach:

text
R32 (16 matches) → R16 (8 matches) → QF (4 matches) → SF (2 matches) → Final (1 match)

On desktop, this could be 5 columns. On mobile, it would stack vertically.

Let me do this with a responsive grid. Each column has matches stacked vertically with spacing to align with the next round's matches.

Actually, I realize I should also handle the edge case where the user has made some R32 predictions, then goes back and changes a group result that changes a qualifier. The R32 prediction that used the old qualifier should be cleared. My invalidation function handles this.

I'm going to write a clean, complete, working file. Let me be methodical.

Let me also think about what happens when a user has NO predictions yet and navigates to R32. They should see a lock message saying "Complete Group Stage first".

And when they have some but not all group predictions, same thing - the R32 requires ALL 72 group matches to be predicted.

For R16: requires all 16 R32 matches to be predicted. For QF: requires all 8 R16 matches. For SF: requires all 4 QF matches. For Final: requires both SF matches.

This is clean and simple.

Now, what about the "update classifieds" feature? When the user goes back to groups and changes a prediction:

  1. The group standings update immediately
  2. If any qualifiers changed, the invalidation cascade runs
  3. Downstream predictions that are now invalid get cleared
  4. A toast tells the user how many predictions were invalidated
  5. The user can then go to the affected phases and re-predict

Actually, I realize for the invalidation to work correctly when changing group predictions, I need to be careful. The current getR32Teams function uses getQualified which depends on ALL group predictions. If the user changes one group match, all qualifiers are recalculated. Even if only one group's qualifiers change, all R32 predictions should be checked.

My invalidation function already does this correctly - it recalculates everything and checks all predictions.

One edge case: what if a team was "Winner of Group A" in R32, and after the group change, "Winner of Group A" is still the same team? Then the R32 prediction is still valid and shouldn't be cleared. My function checks this correctly - it compares the predicted winner against the current teams in the match.

OK, I'm confident the logic is correct. Let me write the code.

Now, truly writing the code:

javascript
// I'll use these helper functions for rendering match cards in the summary:
function renderMatchCard(home, away, winner, idx) {
const homeW = winner === home;
const awayW = winner === away;
return `<div class="bm">
<div class="bt ${homeW ? 'w' : ''}">
<span class="flex-1 truncate">${home}</span>
${homeW ? '<i class="fa-solid fa-caret-right" style="color:var(--accent)"></i>' : ''}
</div>
<div class="bt ${awayW ? 'w' : ''}">
<span class="flex-1 truncate">${away}</span>
${awayW ? '<i class="fa-solid fa-caret-right" style="color:var(--accent)"></i>' : ''}
</div>
</div>`;
}

For the "Lock Now" button: sets the phase's deadline to 1 minute in the past (so it's immediately locked). For the "Remove" button: removes the deadline entirely (only if not yet passed).

Wait, if I set it to 1 minute in the past, then isPhaseLocked returns true immediately. But the user can still click "Remove" to unlock it if I check deadline - Date.now() > -3600000 (1 hour grace period)?

No, that's too complex. Let me use a separate locked boolean:

javascript
// Deadline storage format:
{ groups: { deadline: null, manual: false }, r32: { deadline: 1735689600000, manual: false }, ... }
  • deadline: timestamp or null
  • manual: true if locked manually (can be unlocked), false if locked by deadline (can't be unlocked)

Actually, let me simplify: just use deadline timestamps.

  • No deadline = unlocked
  • Deadline in the future = shows countdown, unlocked
  • Deadline in the past = locked, can be unlocked by removing deadline (user's choice - they're the admin of their own predictions)

Wait, the user said "locking after a certain amount of time" - this implies it should be automatic and permanent. So once a deadline passes, it's locked forever.

But then "Lock Now" should also be permanent. And the user might accidentally lock a phase. Let me add a confirmation dialog for "Lock Now".

OK, here's my final approach:

  • Deadline can be set via datetime picker
  • "Lock Now" button with confirmation → sets deadline to current time
  • Once deadline passes, phase is permanently locked (no unlock)
  • If deadline is in the future, it can be removed (cancel the scheduled lock)
  • Visual countdown shown when deadline is in the future

For the confirmation, I'll use a simple inline confirmation (not alert()): the "Lock Now" button changes to "Are you sure? Yes / No" when clicked.

This is clean and user-friendly. Let me implement it.

Here's the complete World Cup 2026 predictor with per-user persistence, cascade invalidation when qualifiers change, and phase locking by deadline:

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
<title>WC 2026 Predictor</title>
<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Outfit:wght@300;400;500;600;700;800;900&family=Space+Grotesk:wght@300;400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
<script>tailwind.config={theme:{extend:{fontFamily:{display:['Outfit'],body:['Space Grotesk']}}}}</script>
<style>
:root{--bg:#090a0c;--bg2:#111318;--card:#171a21;--brd:#23262f;--brd2:#2e323d;--acc:#d4a853;--acc2:#e0bc6a;--accG:rgba(212,168,83,.1);--grn:#10b981;--grnG:rgba(16,185,129,.12);--red:#ef4444;--redG:rgba(239,68,68,.1);--amb:#f59e0b;--ambG:rgba(245,158,11,.1);--txt:#eae8e3;--txt2:#9ca3af;--mut:#5f6572}
*{box-sizing:border-box;margin:0;padding:0}
body{font-family:'Space Grotesk',sans-serif;background:var(--bg);color:var(--txt);min-height:100vh;overflow-x:hidden}
::-webkit-scrollbar{width:5px}::-webkit-scrollbar-track{background:transparent}::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb{background:var(--brd);border-radius:3px}
.auth-bg{min-height:100vh;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
.orb{position:absolute;border-radius:50%;filter:blur(110px);opacity:.18}
.o1{width:450px;height:450px;background:var(--acc);top:-140px;right:-120px;animation:of1 26s ease-in-out infinite}
.o2{width:350px;height:350px;background:var(--grn);bottom:-120px;left:-100px;animation:of2 22s ease-in-out infinite}
@keyframes of1{0%,100%{transform:translate(0,0)}50%{transform:translate(-90px,70px)}}
@keyframes of2{0%,100%{transform:translate(0,0)}50%{transform:translate(70px,-60px)}}
.acard{background:rgba(23,26,33,.9);backdrop-filter:blur(28px);border:1px solid var(--brd);border-radius:16px;padding:40px;width:100%;max-width:420px;position:relative;z-index:1}
.fi{width:100%;padding:11px 15px;background:var(--bg);border:1px solid var(--brd);border-radius:10px;color:var(--txt);font-family:inherit;font-size:14px;outline:none;transition:.2s}
.fi:focus{border-color:var(--acc);box-shadow:0 0 0 3px var(--accG)}
.fi:disabled{opacity:.45;cursor:not-allowed}
.fi::placeholder{color:var(--mut)}
.btn-p{padding:11px 20px;background:var(--acc);color:#0a0a0a;font-family:'Outfit';font-weight:700;font-size:14px;border:none;border-radius:10px;cursor:pointer;transition:.2s}
.btn-p:hover{background:var(--acc2);transform:translateY(-1px)}.btn-p:active{transform:translateY(0)}
.btn-p:disabled{opacity:.4;cursor:not-allowed;transform:none}
.btn-o{background:none;border:1px solid var(--brd);padding:7px 14px;border-radius:8px;color:var(--txt2);font-family:inherit;font-size:12px;cursor:pointer;transition:.2s}
.btn-o:hover{border-color:var(--brd2);color:var(--txt);background:rgba(255,255,255,.03)}
.btn-d{background:var(--redG);border:1px solid rgba(239,68,68,.25);padding:7px 14px;border-radius:8px;color:var(--red);font-family:inherit;font-size:12px;cursor:pointer;transition:.2s}
.btn-d:hover{background:rgba(239,68,68,.18)}
.btn-g{background:none;border:none;color:var(--acc);cursor:pointer;font-family:inherit;font-size:13px;padding:4px 0;transition:.15s}
.btn-g:hover{color:var(--acc2)}
.sidebar{width:256px;background:var(--bg2);border-right:1px solid var(--brd);display:flex;flex-direction:column;position:fixed;top:0;left:0;bottom:0;z-index:40;transition:transform .3s}

This implements a complete 2026 World Cup prediction site with the following features:

Per-user persistence — Predictions are stored in localStorage keyed by email. Each user has completely separate data. Logging in as a different email loads their own bracket.

Cascade invalidation of classified teams — When you change a group stage result, the system recalculates all qualifiers. Any knockout prediction that now references a team no longer in that slot is automatically cleared, and a warning toast tells you how many predictions were invalidated. You can then go through each knockout phase and re-pick based on the updated qualifiers. This works all the way from groups through to the final.

Phase deadline locking — The "Deadlines" page in the sidebar lets you:

  • Set a future date/time for any phase via a datetime picker
  • See a live countdown timer on both the deadlines page and the sidebar
  • Click "Cancel" to remove a future deadline before it expires
  • Click "Lock Now" (with confirmation) to immediately and permanently lock a phase
  • Once a deadline passes, the phase is permanently locked — all buttons become disabled, a red lock banner appears, and a lock icon shows in the sidebar

Full tournament coverage — 12 groups of 4 teams (48 teams), Round of 32, Round of 16, Quarterfinals, Semifinals, Final, and Third-Place match. Live standings update as you pick group results. The "My Bracket" summary page shows your entire prediction path from groups to champion.

Additional UX — Reset buttons per phase (with inline confirmation, no alert()), a "Reset All" danger zone, progress tracking bar, responsive sidebar with mobile hamburger menu, and toast notifications for all actions.

Can you create all the necessary code, images, and files to create the page on GitHub? Including team logos, an initial image that invites users to register, and everything translated into Latin American Spanish.

  1. All content in Latin American Spanish
  2. Registration/login with email and password (saved by user in localStorage)
  3. Full tournament prediction: Group Stage → Round of 32 → Round of 16 → Quarterfinals → Semifinals → Final + Third Place
  4. Ability to update classified teams at each knockout phase
  5. Lock phases after a certain time (deadline)
  6. Team logos (using flag emojis since we can't have actual image files)
  7. An attractive landing/registration page with an inviting hero image
  8. Everything in a single HTML file ready for GitHub Pages

  • Font Awesome for icons

  • Spanish (Latin American) translations throughout

Let me think about the phase locking mechanism - I'll set deadlines for each phase. Since it's a demo, I'll use relative dates (e.g., group stage locks in 7 days from first prediction, or I can set specific future dates for the 2026 WC).

Actually, for a realistic approach, I'll set the lock dates to be configurable and default to some future dates. Since the actual 2026 WC hasn't happened yet, I'll set dates that make sense:

  • Group Stage: locks June 10, 2026
  • Round of 32: locks June 20, 2026
  • Round of 16: locks June 28, 2026
  • Quarterfinals: locks July 4, 2026
  • Semifinals: locks July 8, 2026
  • Final: locks July 12, 2026

This makes it realistic. But for testing, I could also allow an admin override. Let me keep it simple with fixed dates.

Let me also think about "updating classifieds" - this means when you go to a knockout phase, you should be able to see which teams are advancing and potentially override them if the phase isn't locked yet.

For the hero/landing image, I'll create an impressive CSS-based design with gradients, geometric shapes, and a football/soccer theme since we can't use actual images in a single HTML file. I'll use a canvas-based particle animation for the hero.

I need to be very careful about the completeness of this - it needs to be a fully functional, polished page.

Key features:

  1. Landing page: Beautiful hero with particle animation, registration invitation
  2. Auth system: Register/Login with localStorage persistence
  3. Group Stage: 12 groups × 6 matches = 72 predictions, live standings
  4. Round of 32: 16 matches based on group results
  5. Round of 16: 8 matches
  6. Quarterfinals: 4 matches
  7. Semifinals: 2 matches
  8. Final + 3rd place: 2 predictions
  9. Summary/Braqueta: Complete overview
  10. Phase locking: Each phase has a deadline
  11. Update classifieds: Can change knockout teams if phase not locked

Let me structure this carefully. The file will be large but complete.

Actually, thinking about "updating classifieds to the final keys" - I think the user means that when you complete a knockout phase (e.g., Round of 32), the winners should automatically populate the next phase (Round of 16), and you should be able to go back and change your R32 predictions, which would cascade and update the R16 teams. But once a phase is locked (time expired), you can't change it anymore.

This is a complex cascade system. Let me implement it:

  1. Each knockout prediction stores the MATCH INDEX → WINNER TEAM NAME
  2. When rendering the next phase, we look up who won each match
  3. If the previous phase changes, the next phase's available teams change
  4. If a next-phase prediction references a team that's no longer advancing, it gets cleared
  5. If the phase is locked, predictions are frozen

For the cascade clearing: when you change an R32 prediction, check if any R16 prediction references the old winner. If so, clear it (and cascade down).

This is important for data integrity.

Let me implement all of this now. I'll write clean, well-organized code.

For the hero section, I'll create a canvas with animated particles/shapes in gold/green colors on a dark background, with overlay text.

groups: new Date('2026-06-10T23:59:59'),
r32: new Date('2026-06-20T23:59:59'),
r16: new Date('2026-06-28T23:59:59'),

  • Each knockout phase shows the teams that are supposed to come from the previous phase
  • These are derived automatically from previous predictions
  • If you change a previous prediction, the knockout phase teams update automatically
  • Any downstream predictions that become invalid get cleared with a notification
  • Once a phase is locked by deadline, it's frozen

One more thing - the user said "translated into Latin American Spanish". I need to make sure I use:

  • "Registrar" not "Registrarse" (imperative)
  • "Iniciar sesión" not "Log in"
  • "Pronóstico" not "Predicción" (though both are valid, pronóstico is more common for sports betting/predictions in LatAm)

    • Round of 32 → "Treintaidosavos de final" or "Primera ronda eliminatoria"
    • Round of 16 → "Octavos de final"
    • Quarterfinals → "Cuartos de final"
    • Semifinals → "Semifinales"
    • Final → "Final"
    • Third place → "Tercer puesto"
  • "Clasificados" for qualified teams
  • "Bloqueado" for locked
  • "Fecha límite" for deadline
  • "Guardar" for save
  • "Equipos" for teams
  • "Empate" for draw
  • "Victoria local/visitante" for home/away win

Let me use natural Latin American Spanish throughout.

Actually, let me reconsider the structure. For the "updating classifieds" feature - I think the best approach is:

In knockout phases, show the teams that advance from the previous phase. These are computed from predictions. If the user wants to change which team advances, they go back to the previous phase and change their prediction there. The cascade system will:

  1. Update the computed teams in the next phase
  2. Clear any invalid downstream predictions
  3. Show a toast notification about cleared predictions

This is clean and logical.

For the lock system:

  • Each phase has a deadline date
  • A locked phase shows a lock icon and prevents changes
  • The landing page shows countdown timers
  • The sidebar shows lock status

text

<script>
// === DATOS ===
// Teams, groups, flags, brackets, lock dates

// === ESTADO ===
// user, phase, gTab

// === AUTENTICACIÓN ===

// === RENDERIZADO ===

Let me write it now.

For the hero canvas animation, I'll create floating hexagons (like a soccer ball pattern) in gold with connections between nearby ones, giving a network/constellation effect.

Let me code it all up:

Actually, I want to make sure the cascade system works properly. Here's the logic:

javascript
function cascadeClear(preds, changedPhase) {
// When a prediction in 'changedPhase' changes,
// clear predictions in subsequent phases that reference
// teams that may no longer advance
const phases = ['groups', 'r32', 'r16', 'qf', 'sf', 'final'];
const idx = phases.indexOf(changedPhase);
if (idx < 0 || idx >= phases.length - 1) return;
// Get the teams that would advance from changedPhase
// Compare with what was previously predicted in next phase
// Clear any next-phase predictions that reference old teams
// Then recursively cascade
}

Actually, a simpler approach: when rendering a knockout phase, compute the teams from previous phases. If a prediction in the current phase references a team that doesn't match any computed slot, clear it. Do this on save, not on render.

Let me think about this more carefully...

When user changes a group stage prediction:

  1. Save the group prediction
  2. Recompute qualified teams
  3. Check R32 predictions: each R32 match has home/away teams computed from group results
    • If a user had previously picked a winner for an R32 match, check if that winner still matches one of the two teams in that match
    • If not, clear that R32 prediction
  4. If any R32 predictions were cleared, cascade to R16, then QF, etc.

This is the correct cascade logic. Let me implement it.

javascript
function cascadeAfterChange(preds, fromPhase) {
const phaseOrder = ['groups', 'r32', 'r16', 'qf', 'sf'];
const idx = phaseOrder.indexOf(fromPhase);
if (idx === -1 || idx >= phaseOrder.length) return false;
let changed = false;
const nextPhase = phaseOrder[idx + 1];
if (nextPhase === 'r32') {
const newTeams = getR32Teams(preds);
for (let i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
const pick = preds.r32[i];
if (pick && pick !== newTeams[i].home && pick !== newTeams[i].away) {
delete preds.r32[i];
changed = true;
}
}
} else if (nextPhase === 'r16') {
const r32Teams = getR32Teams(preds);
const newTeams = getNextTeams(preds.r32, r32Teams, R16D);
for (let i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
const pick = preds.r16[i];
if (pick && pick !== newTeams[i].home && pick !== newTeams[i].away) {
delete preds.r16[i];
changed = true;
}
}
}
// ... etc for qf, sf
if (changed) {
cascadeAfterChange(preds, nextPhase);
return true;
}
return false;
}

This is clean. Let me implement it fully.

Now, for the "Final" phase (champion + third place):

  • Champion comes from SF winners
  • Third place comes from SF losers
  • If SF predictions change, check if final/third place picks are still valid

OK, I have a clear picture now. Let me write the complete code.

One more consideration: the user said "allow updating each of the classifieds to the final keys". I think this means that when you're in a knockout phase, you can see and modify which teams are classified/advancing. My cascade system handles this - you go back to the previous phase to change who advances.

But maybe they want the ability to directly pick teams in knockout matches from a dropdown? Let me think...

Actually, I think the simplest and most intuitive approach is:

  • In knockout phases, the two teams for each match are shown (computed from previous phase)
  • User picks the winner
  • If they want to change which teams are in a match, they go back and change the previous phase
  • The cascade handles invalidation

This is standard bracket behavior (like ESPN, FIFA's bracket challenge, etc.)

But wait - maybe the user wants something different. "Actualizar cada uno de los clasificados" could mean they want to be able to manually override which team advances to a match, independent of the bracket logic. Like, in R16 match 1, instead of it automatically being "Winner R32 Match 1 vs Winner R32 Match 9", they want to be able to pick any team.

Hmm, but that would break the bracket logic. I think the standard approach is better. Let me go with automatic qualification from previous phases, with cascade clearing.

Actually, re-reading: "permitir actualizar cada uno de los clasificados hasta las llaves finales" - "allow updating each of the classifieds up to the final brackets". This confirms my interpretation: you update predictions through each phase, and they cascade to the final bracket.

OK, let me write the code now. I'll be comprehensive.

  • What if a group has no predictions yet? Standings show all zeros.

Actually, for the team "logos" - since the user specifically asked for team logos, let me create small SVG/CSS-based badges that look like team crests. Each one will have:

  • A circular background in a color derived from the team name (hash-based)
  • The 3-letter code in white

I'll show both the flag emoji and the badge side by side.

Let me create a function:

javascript
function teamBadge(name) {
const colors = ['#1a3a5c','#2d5a1e','#5c1a1a','#4a1a5c',...];
const color = colors[hashCode(name) % colors.length];
return `<span class="team-badge" style="background:${color}">${AB[name]}</span>`;
}

This gives each team a consistent colored badge. I'll combine it with the flag emoji.

For the canvas animation, I'll create a particle network effect. Here's the approach:

  • 80 particles floating randomly

This creates a constellation/network effect that looks professional and premium.

OK, writing the final code now. No more planning.

Let me think about one more thing: the 48-team format. In 2026:

  • 12 groups of 4 teams
  • Top 2 from each group + 8 best third-placed teams = 32 teams advance
  • Round of 32 → Round of 16 → Quarterfinals → Semifinals → Final + Third place

This is correct. My bracket setup handles this.

The Round of 32 bracket pairing rules (from FIFA):

  • 8 group winners vs 8 best third-placed teams (matches 1-8)
  • 4 group winners vs 4 runners-up (matches 9-12)
  • 4 runners-up vs 4 runners-up (matches 13-16)

Wait, let me double-check. The actual 2026 format:

  • Matches 1-8: Winner A vs 3rd#1, Winner B vs 3rd#2, ..., Winner H vs 3rd#8
  • Matches 9-12: Winner I vs Runner-up A, Winner J vs Runner-up B, Winner K vs Runner-up C, Winner L vs Runner-up D
  • Matches 13-16: Runner-up E vs Runner-up I, Runner-up F vs Runner-up J, Runner-up G vs Runner-up K, Runner-up H vs Runner-up L

Then R16:

  • Match 1 winner vs Match 9 winner
  • Match 2 winner vs Match 10 winner
  • etc.

Wait, that doesn't seem right. Let me look at the actual bracket more carefully.

The R32 bracket for 2026:

text
Match 1: W-A vs 3rd-1
Match 9: W-I vs RU-A
→ R16 Match 1: W1 vs W9

Match 2: W-B vs 3rd-2
Match 10: W-J vs RU-B
→ R16 Match 2: W2 vs W10

Match 3: W-C vs 3rd-3
Match 11: W-K vs RU-C
→ R16 Match 3: W3 vs W11

Match 4: W-D vs 3rd-4
Match 12: W-L vs RU-D
→ R16 Match 4: W4 vs W12

Match 5: W-E vs 3rd-5
Match 13: RU-E vs RU-I
→ R16 Match 5: W5 vs W13

Match 6: W-F vs 3rd-6
Match 14: RU-F vs RU-J
→ R16 Match 6: W6 vs W14

Match 7: W-G vs 3rd-7
Match 15: RU-G vs RU-K
→ R16 Match 7: W7 vs W15

Match 8: W-H vs 3rd-8
Match 16: RU-H vs RU-L
→ R16 Match 8: W8 vs W16

This is what I have. Good.

Then QF:

text
QF1: W-R16-1 vs W-R16-2
QF2: W-R16-3 vs W-R16-4
QF3: W-R16-5 vs W-R16-6
QF4: W-R16-7 vs W-R16-8

SF:

text
SF1: W-QF1 vs W-QF2
SF2: W-QF3 vs W-QF4

Final: W-SF1 vs W-SF2 3rd: L-SF1 vs L-SF2

This all checks out with my bracket definitions. Good.

javascript

'USA':'#002868','France':'#002395','Brazil':'#009c3b','Argentina':'#74acdf',
'Germany':'#000000','Spain':'#c60b1e','England':'#cf081f','Portugal':'#006600',
